title: Crystallised ginger
subtitle: Home-candied ginger slices coated in caster sugar
excerpt: Fresh ginger root blanched three times to remove bitterness, then simmered in a sugar syrup until the slices turn translucent and the syrup is almost entirely absorbed. Dried and rolled in caster sugar. The result is far better than any shop-bought crystallised ginger: fiery, sweet, and properly chewy.
sourceNotes: Crystallised and candied ginger have been made in Britain since at least the seventeenth century, when preserved ginger from Asia and the Caribbean was imported in bulk. Recipes for candied ginger appear in British domestic cookbooks from the eighteenth century onwards, including Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747). The triple-blanching technique to reduce bitterness before candying is documented in confectionery manuals of the nineteenth century. The recipe here follows the established formula.

recipe.batchNotes: Scales straightforwardly. Use a larger saucepan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for up to three months in an airtight jar in a cool, dry place. The flavour deepens over time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required. The ginger is blanched in boiling water three times, then simmered in a sugar syrup on the hob until the syrup reduces and is almost entirely absorbed into the ginger — roughly 45 to 60 minutes of simmering.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fresh ginger is blanched — briefly boiled in water that is then discarded — three times before candying. Each blanch removes some of the sharpest volatile compounds from the ginger, producing a candied result that is fiery but not harsh or raw-tasting. Skipping blanches produces a more bitter, aggressive flavour that some people find unpleasant in sweets. Using the same water for all three blanches defeats the purpose — always start with fresh water.

content.text: is not optional — it separates crystallised ginger that tastes bright and clean from ginger that tastes harsh. The candying itself is slow, low work: the ginger simmers in syrup for almost an hour. Plan the day around it.

attrs.prepNote: peeled and cut into 5 mm slices

content.text: Place the sliced ginger in a saucepan, cover generously with cold water, and bring to the boil. Drain through a sieve and discard the water. Repeat twice more — three blanches in total, always starting with fresh cold water. After the third blanch, drain and set aside.

Combine the 500 g caster sugar and 500 ml water in the saucepan and stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves. Add the blanched ginger and bring to a gentle simmer. Cook uncovered over low heat for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the ginger is translucent throughout and the syrup has reduced to a thick, almost dry coating on the ginger. The ginger is done when almost no liquid remains in the pan. Remove from the heat and set aside to cool in the pan for 10 minutes.

Spread the coating sugar in a shallow bowl. Lift the ginger slices from any remaining syrup and toss a few at a time in the caster sugar until coated on all sides. Arrange on a cooling rack and leave to dry at room temperature for at least 6 hours, or overnight, until the surface is dry and no longer sticky. Store in an airtight jar.
